clean_story:
Nuclear energy without waste. Heat from seawater.

The term cold fusion emerged in 1989 when chemists Martin Fleischmann and Stanley Pons claimed to fuse hydrogen atoms at room temperature—like squeezing two soap bubbles into one, but releasing energy instead of a pop. Most nuclear reactions need extreme heat (millions of degrees) to overcome repulsion between atoms. Their setup? A simple glass jar with palladium electrodes, heavy water (deuterium), and a battery. They reported excess heat—enough to boil the water—and traces of neutrons, hinting at fusion.

Skepticism erupted. Labs worldwide failed to replicate it. Errors surfaced: faulty neutron detectors, miscalibrated thermometers. By 1990, mainstream science dismissed it as "pathological science"—where desire clouds judgment. Yet pockets persisted. The U.S. Navy quietly funded research; Japan’s Technova Inc. reported anomalous heat in 2015 using nickel-hydrogen systems. No one has built a working reactor, but the dream lingers: a tabletop power source running on seawater, no radiation, no meltdowns.

Three related subjects are low-energy nuclear reactions, palladium’s hydrogen sponge effect, and science’s replication crisis.
